questions.prompt: You inherit a Cloudflare setup with SSL mode set to Flexible. The lock icon shows in browsers. What's wrong and what's the fix?

choices.text: Nothing — the lock icon means it's safe.

Flexible mode encrypts user↔Cloudflare but leaves Cloudflare↔origin in plain HTTP. The lock icon misleads users. Switch to Full (strict) and install a Cloudflare Origin Cert at origin. This is one of the most common audit-failures inherited from older setups.

questions.explanation: Flexible is the security anti-pattern: lock icon in browser; plaintext on the back leg. Always audit SSL mode on inherited domains; switch to Full (strict) immediately if Flexible. Cloudflare Origin Cert makes the fix trivial.

questions.prompt: An attacker bypassed your Cloudflare WAF by attacking your origin directly. How did they find the origin IP?

choices.text: Most likely via leak: mail server on the same IP, old DNS history, certificate transparency logs, or subdomain pointing direct. Mitigation: split mail to a different IP, lock origin firewall to Cloudflare's IP ranges only, audit for leaks periodically. Without firewall lockdown, IP hiding is partial.

questions.explanation: Origin IP leaks via mail records, CT logs, and old DNS data are the standard discovery vectors. Firewall lockdown to Cloudflare's IP ranges is the structural defense — even if the IP leaks, attackers can't connect.
